coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-doped CdS microspheres with enhanced photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ying
Yu, Yu-Xiang
Zhang, Wei-De
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-doped CdS (C/CdS) microspheres were successfully synthesized by tuning the concentration of CQDs in the solutions with glutathione as a template. The formation process of the uniform C/CdS microspheres was investigated by time-dependent experiments. The C/CdS exhibits obviously enhanced photocatalytic activity toward degradation of RhB under the solar-simulated light irradiation. In addition, a higher and more stable photocurrent was generated at the C/CdS electrode under visible light irradiation. The significant enhancement in the carrier density and annihilation of fluorescence after incorporation of CQDs into the CdS microspheres further confirm the efficient charge separation and transportation in the composites. The CQDs in the composites can efficiently trap electrons, thus hindering the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
CdS
Doping
Photocatalysis
Ostwald ripening process

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdS and C/CdS microspheres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The formation of CdS spheres at different reaction time (A) 1h; (B) 2h; (C) 3h; (D) 3.5h; (E) 4h; (F) 6h.

(A) SEM image of CdS spheres; (B) high-magnification FESEM image of the CdS spheres; (C and D) SEM images of 5% and 10% C/CdS samples; (E) SEM image of the 50% C/CdS; (F) FESEM image of CdS without glutathione during the hydrothermal process.
(A) TEM and (B) HRTEM images of CdS; (C and D) TEM images of the 50% C/CdS hollow spheres.
XRD patterns of pristine CdS, 10% C/CdS and CQDs samples. Inset, the IR spectra of the CdS and 10% C/CdS.
(A) XPS survey scan over a large energy range, and high resolution spectra of Cd 3d (B), S 2p (C), C 1s (D) of the 10% C/CdS composite.
UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the CdS, C/CdS and CQDs samples. Inset: the optical images of the CdS and 10% C/CdS.
PL spectra of the CdS, 5% and 10% C/CdS samples with excitation wavelength of 560nm.
(A) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB over the prepared catalysts; (B) cycling runs for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over 1% C/CdS.
(A) AC impendence plots of the CdS and C/CdS electrodes; (B) Mott–Schottky plots of the CdS and C/CdS electrodes.

(A) Current–potential curves of the CdS and C/CdS electrodes in 0.50M Na2SO3/Na2S solution under illumination; (B) on–off cycles of the CdS and C/CdS electrodes under Xe lamp illumination in 0.50M Na2SO3/Na2S solution.
Formation mechanism of the CdS spheres.
Schematic diagram of the charge transfer process of the C/CdS composite under visible light irradiation.
Physical properties of the CdS and C/CdS samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-doped CdS microspheres with enhanced photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Guangzhou 510640, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-doped CdS (C/CdS) microspheres were successfully synthesized by tuning the concentration of CQDs in the solutions with glutathione as a template. The formation process of the uniform C/CdS microspheres was investigated by time-dependent experiments. The C/CdS exhibits obviously enhanced photocatalytic activity toward degradation of RhB under the solar-simulated light irradiation. In addition, a higher and more stable photocurrent was generated at the C/CdS electrode under visible light irradiation. The significant enhancement in the carrier density and annihilation of fluorescence after incorporation of CQDs into the CdS microspheres further confirm the efficient charge separation and transportation in the composites. The CQDs in the composites can efficiently trap electrons, thus hindering the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, such carbon materials as graphene nanosheets and carbon nanotubes have been extensively investigated. However, the development of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) remains inchoate. CQDs present a new class of nanocarbons that comprise discrete, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with the size less than 10nm, and surface functionalized with polar carboxyl or hydroxyl groups [1–3]. Compared to fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots like CdS, CdTe, PbS, Si, and CuInS2 [4–9], CQDs display favorable properties such as chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and eco-friendliness. Moreover, CQDs exhibit excellent performance of broad absorption spectrum, large absorption coefficient, and good solubility in polar solvents [10,11]. Thus, CQDs are considered as potential candidates for future electronic devices. Efforts have been focused on synthesis of CQDs by both top-down and bottom-up methods. Laser ablation and electrochemical oxidation are the most widely explored top-down processes [12–14]. Nowadays, the synthesis of CQDs has been successfully conducted by different methods, such as hydrothermal process, microwave synthesis, and oxygen plasma treatment [15–17]. Due to the quantum confinement effect, functions of the CQDs can be tailored by controlling the size for potential applications as luminescent materials, single electron transistors, light-harvesting assemblies, and electron-transporting materials [18–20]. The strong quantum confinement and edge effect [21] also make it possible to use the CQDs in solar cells for efficient charge transfer. It has been demonstrated that because of their strong electron acceptance and fast electron transport properties, carbon nanotubes provide a direct way for the photogenerated charge carriers, leading to the enhancement of photocatalytic performance of the semiconductor/CNTs nanocomposites [22–30]. Thus, in order to achieve superior photocatalytic performance and quantum efficiency, efforts have also been made on the development of nanocomposites based on other nanocarbons and semiconductors [31–34]. Recently, efficient photocatalysts based on CQDs have been achieved. A previous report described the preparation of CQDs-embedded mesoporous hematite as a photocatalyst [35]. The CQDs in the catalysts were considered to play a critical role in improving the photocatalytic activity. Liu and his colleagues developed a quasi-cube hematite/CQDs nanocomposite, and investigated its photocatalytic activity toward degradation of benzene and methanol under visible light irradiation [36]. Kang et al. recently described the fabrication of CQDs/Ag3PO4 and CQDs/Ag/Ag3PO4 photocatalysts. The authors ascribed the enhanced photocatalytic activity and structural stability for the photodecomposition of organic compounds to the unique property including the insoluble, photoinduced electron transfer, up-conversion luminescence and electron reservoir properties of CQDs [37]. Shen et al. constructured a photoanode of CQDs-decorated SrTiO3, which was able to convert near IR photon-energy to photocurrent. A high electron–hole separation rate of the heterojunction between SrTiO3 and CQDs was also achieved [38].

As an important group II–VI semiconductor, cadmium sulfide (CdS) has been extensively studied for applications in optoelectronic devices. The bandgap of CdS is 2.4eV, which matches the solar spectrum for energy conversion well. The size, morphology, and structure of the nanocrystals are critical to determine the optical and electronic properties of CdS [39–43]. Cadmium ion may coordinate with many organic and inorganic ligands to form complexes that are used to prepare CdS nanomaterials. As it is known to all, CdS is not stable because of photocorrosion [44]. One of the most widely concerned photocatalysts is CdS-based photocatalysts which combine CdS with other semiconductors or materials to effectively suppress photocorrosion of CdS [45–48].

Herein, we report the preparation of CQDs-doped CdS microspheres by a simple hydrothermal approach. The as-prepared photocatalysts were examined by characterizing the structure, morphology, and optical properties. The photocatalytic activity of the composites towards the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) was investigated. The photoelectrical activity of the composites towards water splitting was also studied and the results demonstrate that doping of CdS by CQDs significantly improves the photoelectrical efficiency. The CQDs efficiently utilize the full spectrum of sunlight and greatly enhance the photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical activity upon the up-conversion effect. In addition, CQDs can be taken as electron reservoirs which hinder the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes effectively.

L-glutathione was obtained from WAKO, Japan. Cd(NO3)2·4H2O and CH4N2S were obtained from Kermel, Tianjing. Polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mw=20,000) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass was obtained from Nippon Sheet, Japan. FTO (25Ω/sq.) with dimensions of 30×10×2mm3 was cleaned by successive ultrasonication in acetone and ethanol, and then rinsed with deionized water (resistivity >18.4MΩ/cm).

CQDs were prepared by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite. The graphite rods were used as both working electrode and counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl was used as reference electrode. The process of electrochemical exfoliation of graphite was operated in 0.10M C2H5ONa solution by cyclic voltammetry scanning in 0–10V at 0.5V/s. The electrolyte solution was found to change from colorlessness to yellow and finally to dark brown. The CQDs were collected after centrifugation and dialysis with a dialysis bag. Finally, a yellow aqueous solution containing CQDs was obtained. The prepared CQDs solution remained homogeneous after 1month at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdS and C/CdS microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The solution used for preparation of CdS microspheres contains 1.0mmol Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, 3.0mmol thiourea, and 0.60mmol glutathione. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 1h to obtain homogeneity, and then put in a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless-autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 200°C for 3.5h. The sediments were separated by centrifugation and dried at 80°C for 12h. The C/CdS samples were obtained by adding 0.175, 0.35, 0.7, 1.75, 3.5 and 17.5mL CQDs solution. The resulting samples were indicated as 0.5%, 1%, 2%, 5%, 10% and 50% C/CdS, respectively.

The as-synthesized CdS and C/CdS microspheres were thoroughly mixed with polyethylene glycol, which was used as a binder, and then cast on FTO substrate. The film was dried and calcined under the protection of N2 at 400°C for 2h to remove the adhesives and obtain the CdS and C/CdS thin film electrodes.

The products were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on an X’Pert MnPD Pro X-ray diffractometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were recorded on Philips CM 300FE and LEO 1530Vp, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on FEI Tecnai G20 which was operated at 200kV. UV–vis spectroscopy was recorded on a Hitachi U3010 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence measurements were carried out on a Hitachi F-4500 FL spectrophotometer.

The charge transfer resistance was determined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660C, Shanghai, China). The applied potential for the EIS measurement was +0.23V (open circuit potential, V oc); the perturbation amplitude was 10mV and the frequency range was between 100kHz and 0.01Hz. The electrolyte was an aqueous solution containing 0.50M KCl, 0.010M [Fe(CN)6]3−, and 0.010M [Fe(CN)6]4−. The Mott–Schottky analysis was conducted using an impedance-potential spectroscopy at 1000Hz in 0.10M NaOH (pH=12.6) solution by scanning the potential from −1.0 to 0V at a step of 50mV/s. Flat band potential and charge carrier density were calculated from the Mott–Schottky plots.

The photocatalytic degradation of RhB was carried out in an aqueous solution at ambient temperature. 30mg CdS or C/CdS photocatalyst was added into 30mL 1×10−5 mol/L RhB solution. The mixture was put into a cylindrical flask with magnetic stirring in dark for 30min to reach adsorption equilibrium, and then exposed to a 300W tungsten halide lamp with a cutoff filter (λ >400nm). During the irradiation process, 2mL aqueous solution was collected at certain time intervals. The sample solution was centrifuged and then analyzed using a UV–vis spectrometer at a wavelength of 554nm, corresponding to the maximum absorption of RhB.

Photoelectrochemical characterizations were performed in a three-electrode cell made of quartz. Pt sheet and a saturated Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The as-prepared CdS or C/CdS electrode was employed as the working electrode with an area of 0.50×0.50cm2. 0.50M Na2S/Na2SO3 solution was used as the electrolyte and sacrificial reagent. A 300W Xe arc lamp (PLS-SXE 300/300UV) equipped with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ >420nm) was utilized as the visible light. The integrated visible light intensity was 150mW/cm2. Current–voltage curves were recorded by an electrochemical workstation (Ingsens-1030, Guangzhou, China).

Time-dependent experiments clearly reveal the formation process of the CdS spheres. Low-resolution SEM images show the morphology of the products changes upon the increase of the reaction time (Fig. 1 ). A light yellow suspension was formed after 1h reaction, indicating the swift formation of CdS. Fig. 1A depicts the SEM image of the CdS obtained at 1h, showing particles with size ranging from 10 to 50nm. The small particles gradually aggregated and formed spheres as the time prolonged to 3h. The flower-shaped spheres with diameters ranging from 300nm to 1μm were finally formed (Fig. 1D) and the color of the products became deep-yellow as the time prolonged to 3.5h. As the reaction was further proceeding, the surface of the flower-shaped spheres became smooth and the spheres also became smaller. The reason may be that the CdS flower-shaped spheres were partly dissolved in the weak acid solution at high temperature and under high pressure. The acid was gradually generated in the reaction process (Eq. (4)).

The following reactions are believed to be responsible for the formation of CdS spheres:
(1)
Cd
2 +
( aq ) + 2

(2)

)
2

)

)

)

Overall reaction is:
(4)

NH
3

The growth mechanism of CdS spheres through nanoparticles’ self-assembly is also investigated. On the basis of our experimental results, it is proposed that the growth mechanism of the spheres follows the process of thiourea-assisted nucleation growth and glutathione-assisted orientation assembly, and then the formation of the flower-shaped spheres. At the initial reaction stage, free Cd2+ coordinates with thiourea to form Cd(NO3)2·x(NH2)2CS (x =1, 2) complex which further decomposes to CdS nuclei. To explore the role of glutathione, a control experiment was carried out in the absence of glutathione. Without glutathione, aggregated nanoparticles with an average diameter of 50nm were obtained (Fig. 2 F). The result demonstrates that the glutathione serves as a template to orientate the reagents for the formation of flower-shaped CdS spheres, while thiourea plays dual roles: as a ligand and as a source for providing S to form the CdS microspheres.

The morphology of the C/CdS microspheres was observed by SEM (Fig. 2). A low-magnification SEM image indicates that the CdS sample is mainly composed of microspheres (Fig. 2A). Most of them exhibit monodispersive characteristic, along with well-controlled size and structure. From the enlarged SEM image of the CdS (Fig. 2B), the 3D flower-shaped microspheres were assembled by small nanoparticles which shared a common center. The growth tends to form a compact, spherical structure to minimize the surface energy which causes the aggregation, resulting in a flower-shaped sphere. Fig. 2C and D presents the samples with 1.38wt% and 1.89wt% CQDs, which are well-defined spheres with approximately 800nm in diameter. Fig. 2E shows the SEM image of high content of 5.90wt% CQDs in the composite. Some cavities were observed in the center of the microspheres, which provides direct evidence that C/CdS microspheres have a hollow structure with high doping level of CQDs. The results could be elucidated by Ostwald ripening mechanism, which has been used to explain the formation of hollow nanospheres of TiO2 [49], Cu2O [50], Co3O4 [51] and Cd(OH)2 [52].

To further confirm the structure of the samples, TEM images were recorded. The TEM image reveals that the CdS microspheres have an average outer diameter ranging from 200 to 700nm (Fig. 3 A). HRTEM image given in Fig. 3B demonstrates the high crystallinity and lattice of the CdS crystals. The crystal lattice fringes are clearly observed and the measured interplanar distance is in accordance with the (002), (101), and (100) lattices of hexagonal CdS. Fig. 3C and D illustrates that the C/CdS microspheres contain high content of CQDs solution, which reveal the hollow sphere characteristic.

The formation mechanism of the CdS spheres is proposed as Scheme 1 . As is aforementioned, the hollow spheres were formed through the Ostwald ripening process. The diffusion-controlled coarsening leads to the attachment of the small particles to the spheres and the ripening process causes the formation of inter-connected hollow spheres. The CQDs in the C/CdS system promote the formation of hollow spheres. The hollow spheres are generated by high concentration of CQDs in the solution while low concentration of CQDs merely constitutes flower-shaped microspheres. The possible mechanism of the formation of hollow structure is unclear yet, which needs further study in our future work.

The XRD patterns of CQDs, CdS and C/CdS are displayed in Fig. 4 . The CQDs show a broader (002) peak centered at around 18°, indicating the process of electrochemical exfoliation of graphite introduces more active sites on the surface of CQDs, which is further confirmed by IR spectroscopy (Fig. S3 in Supplementary materials). The diffraction peaks of CdS sample can be indexed as hexagonal CdS, with cell constants as a =0. 4316nm and c =0.6713nm (JCPDF Card No. 65-3414). The strong and sharp diffraction peaks indicate that the CdS microspheres were well-crystallized. No peaks of impurities were detected, revealing the high purity of the as-synthesized products. The relative intensity of the (002) peak was stronger than those of the diffraction peaks of (100) and (101), which indicates the high crystalline order along the c-axis. However, with the addition of CQDs, the intensity of (100) and (101) peaks decreases and the intensity ratios of (002)/(100) and (002)/(101) increase, which represent the decrease in crystallinity in the x–y plane and preferential orientation along the c-axis of nanorods [53]. The TEM results visually reveal the width of nanorods was narrowed after incorporation of CQDs. Thus, the flower-shaped microspheres consist of high crystallinity nanorods. Notably, the characteristic diffraction pattern for carbon is too weak to be detected in the C/CdS composites because of the small quantity of C, which needs further examination by elemental analysis (Table 1 ).

Fig. 5 A shows the XPS survey scan over a large energy range at low resolution. Fig. 5B–D shows the high resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d, S 2p and C 1s for the C/CdS sample, respectively. The binding energies of the Cd 3d peaks as well as S 2p peaks are in agreement with the presence of CdS. In the C 1s spectrum, the peak marked by C1 corresponds to the sp2 (284.8eV) coordinated carbon hybridization. The peak denoted by C2 at 286.1eV is assigned to hydroxyl group, and the peak denoted by C3 at 288.8eV is associated with carboxyl group, indicating the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. The results of XPS confirm the CQDs were incorporated into the CdS microspheres.

Diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples are analyzed by Kubelka–Munk function:
F ( R ) = ( 1 - R
)
2
/ 2 R = K / S
where K and S represent the absorption and scattering coefficient, respectively. A significant enhancement of the absorption edge towards longer wavelength (from 550 to 700nm) which indicates a better absorption in visible light region was observed for all C/CdS samples. This result clearly reveals that the onset absorption wavelength of the 10% C/CdS was longer than that of the pristine CdS. This feature confirms that the carbon nanoparticles were successfully introduced into the composites. The color of the samples changes from orange 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 6, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 to brown after incorporation of CQDs into the CdS microspheres (inset of Fig. 6 ). The CQDs which play an important role in utilizing sunlight induce a higher photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts. The CQDs in the C/CdS composites provide effective photo-absorption for visible light-driven applications. Moreover, the CdS only uses the light with wavelength shorter than 560nm to form photogenerated electron/hole pairs, while the up-converted PL property of CQDs [13,36,54] may make C/CdS system utilize more visible light.

The efficiency of charge trapping and recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes in the semiconductor can be verified by photoluminescence (PL) spectrum. Fig. 7 shows the PL spectra of the CdS and C/CdS samples. The result confirms highly efficient interfacial charge transfer between CQDs and CdS. The CQDs in the C/CdS system effectively afford exciton separation interfaces and carrier transporting pathways, leading to the dramatic decrease of PL intensity. The CQDs also provide a facile way to direct the flow of photogenerated charge carriers which leads to the enhancement of photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/CdS composites.

Photocatalytic activity is strongly dependent on the transportation and separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes in the catalysts. In order to investigate the visible light photocatalytic performance of the C/CdS samples toward the degradation of organic pollutants, RhB was selected as a model contaminant for photocatalytic decolorization. The solution was magnetically stirred in dark for 30min to equilibrate the adsorption and desorption process prior to the irradiation. Fig. 8 A shows the change in the absorption spectra of RhB aqueous solution which was exposed to visible light in the presence of CdS or C/CdS samples. After irradiation of visible light for 1h, the degradation efficiency of RhB was found to be about 50% and 90% with the assistance of CdS and 1% C/CdS samples, respectively. Obviously, the CQDs in the C/CdS composites are beneficial for the degradation of dye.

In addition to photocatalytic activity, stability of the photocatalysts is another important consideration for practical applications. The stability of the C/CdS for recycle was also studied and the result is illustrated in Fig. 8B. The stability of the 1% C/CdS was examined for degradation of RhB for three cycles, which still revealed high degradation efficiency, indicating that C/CdS composites are well-reproductive catalysts. The C/CdS composites settled down in the dispersion after natural settlement for 5min, which indicates that the CdS spheres can be easily separated for recycle.

When CQDs are attached to the CdS, the matched band edge permits the electrons to transfer from CdS surface to CQDs, which is favorable for the charge separation and stabilization [13]. The photogenerated electron and hole pairs then react with the adsorbed oxidants/reductants (O2/OH−) to produce active
O2− and
OH radicals which subsequently cause degradation of RhB (Scheme 2 ). These results clearly demonstrate that introducing CQDs into CdS spheres can greatly enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 9 A shows the AC impendence of CdS and C/CdS electrodes in 0.50M KCl solution containing 0.010M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at the frequency varying from 100kHz to 0.01Hz. The values of charge transfer resistance (R CT) were recorded at open circuit voltage. A single semicircle at the high frequency region and a straight line at the low frequency region indicate a mixed charge transfer and charge diffusion process [55]. The R CT values of the C/CdS electrodes were much smaller than that of the CdS electrode in dark, which illustrates that CQDs lead to a much lower charge transport resistance and much higher separation efficiency of electrons and holes.

Fig. 9B shows the Mott–Schottky plots for the CdS and C/CdS electrodes in 0.10M NaOH solution. The flat band potential (E fb) is obtained from the intercept of Mott–Schottky plot using the following equation:
1 /
C
2
= 2 [ ( E -
E
fb
) - kT / e ] / ε
ε
o
eN
d
A
2

The values of carrier density (N d) and flat band potential are listed in Table 1. The majority carrier donor concentrations of the C/CdS electrodes calculated from the Mott–Schottky analysis gradually increase compared with the CdS electrode, which vividly indicates an effective separation of electrons and holes. The C/CdS electrodes which reveal high separation efficiency and low recombination efficiency of photogenerated electron and hole pairs hold a great promise for applications in photoelectrochemical cell.

Fig. 10 A shows a set of linear sweep voltammagrams which were recorded at the CdS and C/CdS electrodes in dark and under illumination. All electrodes showed small dark current density and pronounced photoresponse under illumination. Obviously, all the C/CdS samples exhibited substantially enhanced photocurrent compared to the CdS, which can be attributed to the improved visible light absorption by CQDs, as indicated in UV–vis spectra. The photocurrent density (I p) of CdS electrode at +0.23V vs Ag/AgCl is ca. 0.5mA/cm2, and the photocurrent response reached saturation immediately under illumination. Significantly, the 1% C/CdS showed a maximum photocurrent density of 1.4 mA/cm2 at +0.23V, which is about three times larger than that of pristine CdS sample. Meanwhile, Fig. 10A illustrates that the onset potentials of all the C/CdS electrodes were all negatively shifted compared to the CdS electrode. The onset potential of the 1% C/CdS electrode was −1.1V and that of the CdS electrode was only about −0.5V. The negative shift of onset potentials illustrates that the carrier transport is more efficient, thus the photoelectrochemical response is improved.

The photocurrent response to on–off cycles of illumination shows the stability and reproducibility of the observed photocurrent of the CdS electrode (Fig. 10B). The maximum photocurrent and applied voltage generated in the photoelectrochemical cell with >420nm and 150mW/cm2 Xe lamp white light illumination were 0.25 mA/cm2 and 0V, respectively. It was observed that the photoresponse of the C/CdS electrodes showed much higher photosensitivity. The photocurrent observed for the 1% C/CdS electrode was two orders of magnitude higher than the current generated by the CdS electrode. Photoelectrochemical study demonstrates that C/CdS can effectively generate electrons and holes under visible light irradiation and the quantum efficiency is significantly improved after doping. At low doping level of CQDs, the CQDs can absorb visible light and emit short wavelength light through up-conversion, which in turn excites CdS to form electron–hole pairs. Meanwhile, CQDs can also act as electron reservoirs to trap electrons emitted from CdS, resulting in hindrance of the recombination of electron–hole pairs. Upon the increase of the CQDs, the morphology of the C/CdS samples changes, thus influences the photoelectrochemical activity of the samples. In short, C/CdS thin films can be considered as a good candidate for the photoelectrochemical energy conversion device.

In summary, self-assembled flower-shaped CdS microspheres were prepared by a facile hydrothermal route. The possible mechanism for the formation of CdS microspheres by nanoparticles’ self-assembly was proposed. As a novel electron acceptor, the CQDs were integrated into the CdS microspheres to improve the properties of CdS. Compared to the single phase CdS, the C/CdS composite showed higher photocatalytic activity and higher photodegradation rate of RhB, which may be attributed to the electrons trapped by the CQDs and the hindrance to the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs. Meanwhile, the up-converted PL property of CQDs could make the C/CdS photocatalysts utilize the visible light more efficiently; therefore the photocatalytic activity was greatly enhanced. The finding may shed new light on how to design and synthesize novel photocatalysts through combination of CQDs with other nanospecies for photocatalytic reactions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21273080, 21003051 and 21043005) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: More experimental details and results are available in the Supplementary Materials. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2013.03.202.
